claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England

expected_eligibility.name: Blake

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**First question: How old is your child?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** are automatically considered qualifying children
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about two of my children. 

My daughter Alex is 11 years old, and my son Blake is 15. Can I claim for both of them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Yes, let's look at both Alex and Blake.

Since both **Alex (11 years old)** and **Blake (15 years old)** are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification category based on age. However, I need to check a few more requirements to confirm their eligibility.

Let me check the next step for children under 16:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake working 24 hours or more per week in paid work?**

This is just to confirm they meet the work requirements. For children under 16, this is rarely an issue, but I need to verify to ensure they qualify.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 11, so she's just in primary school, and Blake doesn't have a job either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Neither of them are working, so that's fine. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking about benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits or income support

These would be benefits claimed in their own names, not benefits you receive for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you. Let me continue checking the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are either Alex or Blake enrolled in an apprenticeship program?**

This includes:
- Apprenticeships in England (all types)
- Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland
- Apprenticeships in Wales (except Foundation level, which is approved)

Given their ages (11 and 15), this is unlikely, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. They're too young for that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes sense. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status as the claimant.

**Question: Do you normally live in the UK?**

To qualify for Child Benefit, you need to meet one of several residency requirements. The most common is simply that you normally live in the UK. 

Other ways you can qualify include:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions)
- Living abroad as a Crown servant
- Living in certain qualifying countries

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since you're living abroad, I need to check if you meet any of the special circumstances that would still allow you to claim.

**Follow-up question: Are you living abroad in any of the following capacities?**

1. **Crown servant** - working for the UK government abroad (e.g., diplomatic service, armed forces, civil service)?

2. **Living in certain qualifying countries** - there are specific agreements with some countries that may allow you to claim?

Or do any of these apply:
3. **EU Settlement Scheme status** - Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme and meet certain conditions (working, studying, or having sufficient resources)?

Please let me know if any of these situations apply to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I'm just living abroad - not working for the UK government or anything like that, and I don't have any special settlement status.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, based on the information you've provided, this means you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I need to provide you with the eligibility determination now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Eligibility Assessment Result: NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake at this time.

### **Summary for Your Children:**

**Alex (11 years old):** ❌ Not Eligible
- Alex meets all the child-specific requirements (right age, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship)
- However, because you don't meet the UK residency requirements as the claimant, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex

**Blake (15 years old):** ❌ Not Eligible  
- Blake also meets all the child-specific requirements (right age, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship)
- However, because you don't meet the UK residency requirements as the claimant, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake

### **Why You're Not Eligible:**

To claim Child Benefit, you must meet the **UK residency requirements**. This means you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, **OR**
- Have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with additional conditions), **OR**
- Be a Crown servant living abroad, **OR**
- Be living in certain qualifying countries

Since you're living abroad and none of these special circumstances apply to you, you don't meet the residency requirement.

### **What This Means:**

Even though both Alex and Blake would qualify as eligible children if you were living in the UK, the residency requirement is mandatory for the claimant (you) to receive Child Benefit.

### **Possible Next Steps:**

- If your circumstances change and you return to live in the UK, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for both children at that time
- If you believe you should qualify under special circumstances, you may want to contact HMRC directly to discuss your specific situation

I understand this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions about this decision or your circumstances change, please feel free to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 11 years old and meets all the child-related qualifying criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit, as they are living abroad and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, EU Settlement Scheme status, or living in certain qualifying countries). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old and meets all the child-related qualifying criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship). However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit, as they are living abroad and do not qualify under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, EU Settlement Scheme status, or living in certain qualifying countries). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children, Alex (11) and Blake (15), who both meet all the child-specific qualifying criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant is living abroad and does not meet the UK residency requirements, which is a mandatory condition for claiming Child Benefit. The claimant is not a Crown servant, does not have EU Settlement Scheme status, and is not living in a qualifying country. Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
